title: Level Order Traversal
description: The breadth-first traversal, finally: visiting every node level by level using a queue instead of recursion.
objectives: Understand why recursion alone doesn't naturally give level-by-level order
Implement level order traversal using a queue
Trace the algorithm step by step
Recognize level order as the tree-specific case of BFS

sections.text: Preorder, Inorder, and Postorder were all depth-first — commit to one path, backtrack later. Level Order is the breadth-first approach previewed in Tree Traversals: visit every node at the current depth before moving to the next.
2. Why Recursion Alone Doesn't Give This

Recall Recursion's call stack (from Stack Memory): a recursive call dives all the way down one path before returning — inherently depth-first behavior. Getting a level-by-level order needs a fundamentally different tool: a structure that processes things in the order they were discovered, which is exactly what a queue (FIFO, from Queue) provides.

sections.title: The core idea
steps: While the queue isn't empty: pop the front node, visit it, push its children (left, then right) onto the back
Because it's FIFO, all of one level gets processed before any of the next level even starts

sections.title: Tracing it
points: q = [1]. Pop 1, visit it, push its children → q = [2, 3]
Pop 2, visit it, push its children → q = [3, 4, 5]
Pop 3, visit it, no children to push → q = [4, 5]
Pop 4, visit it → q = [5]. Pop 5, visit it → q = []
Result: 1 2 3 4 5 — level by level: root, then both of level 1, then both of level 2

sections.title: Announcing an org chart one entire rank at a time
sections.text: Recall the org chart. Level order is announcing the CEO, then EVERY VP together (before any director), then EVERY director together (before any manager) — rank by rank, never diving into one VP's entire branch before acknowledging the other VPs. That strict rank-by-rank order is exactly what a queue naturally enforces.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Trying to write level order recursively the same way as the other three orders — it fundamentally needs a queue, not the call stack, to get level-by-level behavior.
Forgetting to check for nullptr before pushing a child — pushing null children would need extra guards later, or crash on dereference.
Using a stack instead of a queue — that would produce a different, depth-first-flavored order, not level order.
Forgetting the initial nullptr check for an empty tree — levelOrder(nullptr) should simply do nothing.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is level order traversal?
questions.answer: A breadth-first traversal that visits every node at the current depth/level before moving on to the next level.

questions.question: Why can't level order traversal be implemented the same recursive way as preorder/inorder/postorder?
questions.answer: Because recursion naturally dives depth-first down one path before backtracking; level order needs a queue to process nodes in the order they were discovered, level by level.

questions.question: Describe the algorithm for level order traversal.
questions.answer: Push the root into a queue; while the queue isn't empty, pop the front node, visit it, and push its non-null children onto the back — FIFO order naturally processes each level fully before the next begins.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Level order traversal is the breadth-first approach previewed in Tree Traversals: visiting every node at the current level before moving to the next. Unlike the depth-first orders, it can't be written with simple recursion, since recursion naturally dives down one path first — instead, it uses a queue: push the root, then repeatedly pop the front node, visit it, and push its children onto the back, letting FIFO order naturally process one full level before the next begins.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Level Order Traversal
sections.text: Level order = announce one entire rank before moving to the next. Needs a QUEUE, not recursion — FIFO naturally enforces level-by-level order.
LeetCode: Level Order Traversal in Practice

sections.title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
sections.problemStatement: Given the root of a binary tree, return its level order traversal as a list of lists — one inner list per level.
hints: This is this lesson's own BFS pattern, with one addition: values need to be grouped by level, not flattened into one list.
Before processing each round, record the queue's CURRENT size — that size is exactly how many nodes belong to the current level, since only this level's nodes are in the queue at that moment.
Process exactly that many nodes (collecting their values into one inner list), enqueueing their children as usual, before moving to the next level.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Track each node's depth alongside it in the queue, grouping by depth into a map afterward.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n) for the map

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Standard BFS, snapshotting the queue's size at the start of each level to know exactly how many nodes to process.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the queue and result

sections.title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal II
sections.problemStatement: Given the root of a binary tree, return its level order traversal from LEAF level up to ROOT level (bottom-up).
hints: This is LC 102's exact algorithm — the only difference is the final ORDER of the levels, not how they're computed.
Build the levels top-down exactly as before, since a queue can only naturally process shallowest-first.
Reverse the final list of levels at the very end to present them bottom-up.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Track depth in the queue, group by depth into a map, then read the map from highest depth down to 0.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: This lesson's own BFS level-by-level build, with a single reverse() at the very end.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the queue and result
